name: Hoderi
description: Hoderi, also known as Hoori, is a significant figure in Japanese mythology, primarily known for his role in the lineage of the Imperial family. He is regarded as the elder brother of Hoori (also known as Hikohaho-demi or Yamasachi-hiko), the ancestor of Emperor Jimmu. Hoderi is often associated with the sea and fishing, contrasting with his brother who is linked to hunting. The mythic narrative surrounding Hoderi revolves around the loss of Hoori's fishhook and the subsequent quest to retrieve it, leading to interactions with the sea god Watatsumi. This episode highlights themes of sibling rivalry, cosmic order, and the divine origins of the Japanese imperial line. Hoderi's eventual submission to his younger brother Hoori is interpreted as a symbolic representation of the subjugation of earlier, perhaps indigenous, powers to the Yamato clan, who would eventually establish the imperial dynasty. He is an important, if sometimes overlooked, figure in understanding the complex tapestry of Japanese creation myths and ancestral narratives.
subtitle: Elder Brother of the Imperial Ancestor

keyMyths.title: The Lost Fishhook
keyMyths.description: Hoori borrows his brother Hoderi's fishhook but loses it in the sea. Hoderi demands its return, leading Hoori to descend to the sea floor and seek the help of the sea god Watatsumi. Watatsumi helps Hoori find the hook and provides him with magical jewels to control the tides, ultimately allowing Hoori to overcome Hoderi. This myth establishes the divine lineage of the imperial family and symbolizes the subjugation of the sea to the land.

keyMyths.title: Meeting Princess Toyotama
keyMyths.description: While in the underwater palace of Watatsumi, Hoori meets and marries Princess Toyotama, the sea god's daughter. They live together for several years, during which time Hoori possesses the power and knowledge of the sea. This union further solidifies the connection between the imperial line and the divine realm, specifically the realm of the sea.

keyMyths.title: Hoderi's Submission
keyMyths.description: After Hoori returns to land with the magical jewels, he uses them to subdue Hoderi. Hoderi acknowledges Hoori's superiority and pledges allegiance. This event is interpreted as the symbolic submission of the sea people (or earlier inhabitants of Japan) to the Yamato clan, the ancestors of the imperial family. The subjugation reinforces the legitimacy of the imperial rule.

keyMyths.title: Ancestor of the Hayato
keyMyths.description: Following his submission, Hoderi is said to have become the ancestor of the Hayato people, a group known for their distinctive culture and often associated with the southern regions of Japan. This connection suggests a complex relationship between the imperial court and regional powers, with Hoderi representing a lineage that was incorporated into the broader imperial narrative.
keyMyths.source: Various Shinto Shrines and Local Legends

associations.name: Sea
associations.significance: Hoderi is strongly associated with the sea, reflecting his role as a fisherman and ruler of the marine realm.

associations.name: Fish
associations.significance: Represents Hoderi's connection to the sea and his identity as a skilled fisherman.

associations.name: Fishing
associations.significance: Fishing is a central aspect of Hoderi's identity and the myths associated with him.

associations.name: Blue
associations.significance: Symbolizes the sea and Hoderi's dominion over it.

associations.name: Moon
associations.significance: In some interpretations, the moon is linked to water deities, subtly connecting to Hoderi's marine associations.

associations.name: Brotherhood
associations.significance: His relationship with his younger brother Hoori is a key element in the myths.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings to the Sea
worshipPractices.description: While specific dedicated shrines to Hoderi are not widely documented, offerings to the sea, often including fish and other marine products, can be seen as a form of indirect reverence, acknowledging his dominion over the ocean.

festivals.name: Sea Festivals
festivals.description: Local sea festivals (like those for safe fishing) may implicitly honor Hoderi as a sea deity, though not explicitly named after him. These festivals involve prayers for bountiful catches and safety at sea.

cultural.modernLegacy: Hoderi's story remains relevant in Japanese folklore and historical narratives. He is often referenced in discussions about the Imperial lineage and the connection between the Yamato clan and maritime traditions. He appears in some modern media.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Hoderi's origins are rooted in the early mythological accounts of Japan, particularly the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki. He is the elder brother of Hoori (also known as Yamasachi-hiko), both descendants of the deity Ninigi-no-Mikoto, who was sent to pacify Japan. Hoderi's birth is not explicitly detailed with extraordinary circumstances, but he is presented as a natural continuation of the divine lineage. His role is immediately defined by his skill and connection to the sea, contrasting with his brother's affinity for the land and hunting. This inherent difference sets the stage for their later interactions and the crucial narrative that links them to the Imperial family's ancestry. He embodies the maritime aspect of early Japanese society, reflecting the importance of fishing and the sea in their culture and economy.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: The most prominent myth involving Hoderi revolves around the loss of his fishing hook and its subsequent recovery. Hoderi loans his fishing hook to his younger brother Hoori, who is unsuccessful in using it. To make matters worse, Hoori loses the hook. Hoderi demands its return, leading to conflict between the brothers. Hoori eventually descends to the sea palace of Ryūjin, the dragon king, where he discovers the hook stuck in a fish. After a period of prosperity in the sea palace, Hoori returns with the hook and advice from Ryūjin on how to manage his brother's anger. Following Ryūjin's instructions, Hoori overwhelms Hoderi, who then submits to his younger brother. This myth highlights themes of sibling rivalry, the relationship between land and sea, and the establishment of the Imperial lineage through Hoori.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Hoderi does not have a widespread, dedicated cult following in the same way as some other major deities in Japanese mythology. However, his significance is acknowledged through his role in the Imperial lineage and his association with the sea. While specific shrines dedicated solely to Hoderi are rare, he may be venerated indirectly in shrines dedicated to broader maritime deities or those associated with the Imperial family's ancestors. Offerings related to fishing and the sea, such as prayers for safe voyages and bountiful catches, could be interpreted as a form of respect towards Hoderi. His presence is more prominent in the narrative and symbolic landscape of Japanese mythology than in direct, formalized worship.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Hoderi's primary legacy lies in his role as the elder brother of Hoori, the ancestor of Emperor Jimmu, the first emperor of Japan. Through this connection, Hoderi is indirectly linked to the Imperial family's divine origins and the legitimacy of their rule. His association with the sea also contributes to the broader understanding of early Japanese culture and its relationship with maritime activities. While not a central figure of direct worship, Hoderi's story continues to be retold and reinterpreted in modern media, including literature, theater, and popular culture. His character often serves as a symbol of the complex relationship between tradition, lineage, and the natural world in Japanese identity.

shortDescription: Hoderi, also known as Hoori, is a Japanese sea deity and fisherman. Elder brother of Hoori, he is part of the lineage of the Imperial family.
